label: 2 Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints.pptx

params.file: 2 Relational Data Model and Relational Database Constraints.pptx

title: Relational Model Concepts and Constraints

questions.text: Who is credited with first proposing the relational model of data in 1970?
questions.explanation: The relational model was first proposed by Dr. E.F. Codd of IBM Research in his 1970 paper 'A Relational Model for Large Shared Data Banks'.

questions.text: Match the informal term **Table** to its formal relational model equivalent.
questions.explanation: Informally, a relation looks like a table of values.

questions.text: In the formal relational model, what is a **row** called?
questions.explanation: In the formal relational model, rows are referred to as tuples.

questions.text: What does a **column header** represent in the formal relational model?
questions.explanation: Column headers are formally called attribute names or simply attributes.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a **Domain**?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A set of atomic values that an attribute can take

questions.explanation: A domain is a set of valid values (and a data type/format) from which an attribute derives its values.

questions.text: A relation schema is denoted by \( R(A_1, A_2, ..., A_n) \). What does \( R \) represent?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The domain of the first attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: A specific row in the table

questions.explanation: In the notation \( R(A_1, A_2, ..., A_n) \), \( R \) is the name of the relation.

questions.text: A tuple is defined as an **ordered set** of values. How is a 4-tuple represented?
questions.explanation: Formally, a tuple is an ordered set of values enclosed in angled brackets \( < ... > \).

questions.text: If \( dom(A_1) = \{0, 1\} \) and \( dom(A_2) = \{a, b, c\} \), what is the total number of possible combinations in the Cartesian product \( dom(A_1) \times dom(A_2) \)?
questions.explanation: The Cartesian product includes all possible combinations: (2 values in A1) * (3 values in A2) = 6.

questions.text: The **Relation State** \( r(R) \) is formally defined as a subset of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The set of all attribute names

choices.label: B
choices.text: The Cartesian product of the domains of its attributes

choices.label: C
choices.text: The union of all primary keys

questions.explanation: The relation state \( r(R) \) is a subset of the Cartesian product of the domains: \( r(R) \subseteq dom(A_1) \times dom(A_2) \times ... \times dom(A_n) \).

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding the **ordering of tuples** in a relation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tuples must be ordered by the primary key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tuples must be ordered by the date they were inserted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tuples in a relation are not considered to be ordered.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tuples are ordered alphabetically based on the first attribute.

questions.explanation: In a relation, which is a set of tuples, there is no inherent ordering among the tuples.

questions.text: In the formal relational model, values in a tuple are considered **atomic**. What does this mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They can be divided into smaller sub-components.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They must be unique across the entire table.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are indivisible units of data.

questions.explanation: Values are considered atomic, meaning they are indivisible in the context of the basic relational model.

questions.text: What special value is used to represent unknown or inapplicable data in a tuple?
questions.explanation: A special NULL value is used for unknown or inapplicable data.

questions.text: Which notation refers to the value of attribute \( A_i \) for a specific tuple \( t \)?
questions.explanation: We refer to component values by \( t[A_i] \) or \( t.A_i \).

questions.text: What is a **Superkey**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A set of attributes that can be null.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A set of attributes where no two tuples have the same value.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A single attribute that is always a number.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A foreign key that points to a super relation.

questions.explanation: A superkey is a set of attributes \( SK \) such that no two distinct tuples in any valid state have the same value for \( SK \).

questions.text: A **Key** is defined as a 'minimal' superkey. What does 'minimal' mean in this context?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It contains the fewest possible number of characters.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Removing any attribute from it results in a set that is no longer a superkey.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the primary key with the smallest numeric value.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It must consist of only one attribute.

questions.explanation: A key is minimal if the removal of any attribute from the key destroys the uniqueness property.

questions.text: In the relation schema `CAR(State, Reg#, SerialNo, Make, Model, Year)`, if `{State, Reg#}` is a key, which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: Any set of attributes that includes a key is a superkey.

questions.text: When a relation has multiple candidate keys, the one chosen to uniquely identify tuples is called the:
questions.explanation: One candidate key is chosen arbitrarily to be the primary key.

questions.text: How is the **Primary Key** represented in a relational schema diagram?

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is followed by an asterisk.

questions.explanation: Primary key attributes are traditionally underlined in relational schemas.

questions.text: The **Entity Integrity Constraint** states that:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primary key attributes cannot have null values.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every table must have a foreign key.

questions.explanation: Entity integrity ensures that primary key values are used to identify individual tuples, so they cannot be null.

questions.text: **Referential Integrity** involves a relationship between two relations. The attribute in the referencing relation is called the:
questions.explanation: Attributes in a referencing relation that reference a primary key in a referenced relation are called foreign keys.

questions.text: According to the referential integrity constraint, a foreign key value can be which of the following? (Select the most complete answer)

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only a value existing as a primary key in the referenced relation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Either a value existing as a primary key in the referenced relation OR NULL.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any value that fits the domain.

questions.explanation: Referential integrity requires the FK to either match an existing PK in the referenced table or be NULL (provided the FK is not part of its own PK).

questions.text: Referential integrity constraints are displayed in a schema diagram as:

choices.label: C
choices.text: A directed arc (arrow) from the foreign key to the referenced primary key.

questions.explanation: A referential integrity constraint is displayed as a directed arc from \( R_1.FK \) to \( R_2.PK \).

questions.text:
Consider the following Mermaid diagram representing a relationship. Which type of constraint does the arrow represent?

```mermaid
graph LR
  A[ENROLL.SSN] -- Foreign Key --> B[STUDENT.SSN]
```
questions.explanation: Arrows pointing from one table's attribute to another's primary key represent referential integrity (foreign key) constraints.

questions.text: Which of the following is an **implicit** constraint in the relational model?
questions.explanation: The domain constraint is an implicit constraint requiring every value in a tuple to be from the domain of its attribute.

questions.text: What are **Semantic Integrity Constraints**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Constraints that check if a PK is NULL.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Constraints based on application logic (e.g., max work hours) that cannot be expressed by the model itself.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Constraints that ensure foreign keys point to valid primary keys.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constraints that define data types like VARCHAR or INTEGER.

questions.explanation: Semantic integrity constraints are based on application semantics and usually require a constraint specification language or triggers/assertions.

questions.text: If a database operation violates an integrity constraint, the system might reject the operation. This action is known as:
questions.explanation: The RESTRICT or REJECT option cancels the operation that causes the violation.

questions.text: Which action automatically propagates an update or deletion to all referencing tuples?
questions.explanation: The CASCADE option triggers additional updates so that the violation is corrected by propagating the change.

questions.text: An **INSERT** operation can potentially violate which of the following constraints?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Any of the constraints (Domain, Key, Entity Integrity, Referential Integrity)

questions.explanation: INSERT may violate any of the constraints if the new data is invalid, non-unique, has a null PK, or an invalid FK.

questions.text: A **DELETE** operation on a 'referenced' relation can typically violate which constraint?
questions.explanation: DELETE may violate referential integrity if the primary key value of the tuple being deleted is still referenced by other tuples.

questions.text: Updating an **ordinary attribute** (one that is neither a primary key nor a foreign key) can only violate:
questions.explanation: Updating an ordinary attribute cannot affect tuple identity or references; it only checks if the new value belongs to the domain.

questions.text: Which of the following is true about **Relational Database Schemas** \( S \)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a set of tuples from one table.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a set of relation schemas \( \{R_1, R_2, ..., R_n\} \) belonging to the same database.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It represents the current populated state of the database.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It must always contain exactly 6 relations.

questions.explanation: A relational database schema \( S \) is a set of relation schemas that belong to the same database.

questions.text: A **surrogate key** is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A foreign key that points to itself.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An artificial key like a row-id or sequential number assigned to identify rows.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A key that is made up of all attributes in the relation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A key that is only used for encryption.

questions.explanation: Artificial or surrogate keys are sequential numbers or row-ids assigned to identify rows uniquely.

questions.text: If a relation schema is \( CUSTOMER(Cust-id, Cust-name, Address, Phone\#) \), and a tuple is \( <632895, "John Smith", "101 Main St", NULL> \), this is a:
questions.explanation: It is a 4-tuple because it contains four values, even if one is NULL.

questions.text: True or False: A relation is a set of tuples, and therefore all tuples in a relation must be distinct.
questions.explanation: Formally, a relation is a set of tuples. In set theory, all elements of a set must be distinct.

questions.text: The rule 'Choose as primary key the smallest of the candidate keys' is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A strict formal requirement of the relational model.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A general rule of thumb but sometimes subjective.

questions.explanation: While it is a general rule to choose the smallest key, it is not always applicable and the choice can be subjective.

questions.text: If you update a **Primary Key**, which other operations is it similar to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A DELETE followed by an INSERT.

questions.explanation: Updating a primary key is logically equivalent to deleting the old tuple and inserting a new one with the updated key.

questions.text: In the relation `STUDENT(SSN, Name, Major, Bdate)`, `SSN` is underlined. Which constraint prohibits two students from having the same `SSN`?
questions.explanation: Key constraints ensure that no two tuples have the same value for the key attributes.

questions.text: What is the relation between a Key and a Superkey?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A key can have duplicate values, but a superkey cannot.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no relation between them.

questions.explanation: Any key is a superkey, but not every superkey is a key (a superkey is only a key if it is minimal).

questions.text: What does the term **'Relation State'** (or population) refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The set of attribute names and domains.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The set of all possible tuples.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A specific set of tuples at a given point in time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The integrity constraints applied to the table.

questions.explanation: The relation state \( r(R) \) is a specific 'value' or population of the relation R, containing a set of tuples.

questions.text: Consider the following schema: `ENROLL(SSN, Course#, Quarter, Grade)`. If `SSN` references `STUDENT(SSN)`, and we delete a student from the `STUDENT` table, which action would set the `SSN` in `ENROLL` to NULL?
questions.explanation: The SET NULL option sets the foreign keys of referencing tuples to NULL when the referenced tuple is deleted.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the relationship between the formal model and SQL?

choices.label: B
choices.text: SQL is the formal mathematical model.

choices.label: C
choices.text: SQL is a standard practical model that differs from the formal model in several ways.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The formal model is used only in SQL-99.

questions.explanation: In practice, there is a standard model based on SQL, which has several important differences from the formal model.

questions.text: What is the **Relational Database State**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The collection of all relation schemas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A union of all the individual relation states at a given time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The data type of the primary key.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The list of all foreign key constraints.

questions.explanation: The relational database state is the union of the states of all individual relations in the database.

questions.text: Identify the primary key in the following schema representation: **STUDENT**(**SSN**, Name, Major, Bdate)
questions.explanation: SSN is underlined, which designates it as the primary key.

questions.text: In the context of referential integrity, what is the **referenced relation**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The relation containing the foreign key.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The relation whose primary key is being pointed to.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The relation that was most recently updated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A table with no primary key.

questions.explanation: The referenced relation \( R_2 \) is the one that contains the primary key being referred to by the foreign key of relation \( R_1 \).

questions.text: Based on the provided student enrollment exercise, which table would contain a foreign key referencing `COURSE(Course#)`?
questions.explanation: The `ENROLL` table contains `Course#` to track student enrollment, making it a foreign key referencing the `COURSE` table.
